title: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases
section: The Immune System in Health and Disease

items.title: Understanding Primary Immunodeficiencies (PIDs) and Their Classification
items.content: Primary immunodeficiencies are genetic disorders that impair the immune system, leading to increased susceptibility to infections. They can affect any part of the immune response, from innate to adaptive immunity.
keyPoints: Hundreds of gene products mediate immune responses; impairment leads to PIDs.
PIDs have Mendelian inheritance with over 550 conditions identified.
Deleterious mutations in approximately 500 genes are known.
Prevalence is estimated at 5–10 per 100,000 individuals, likely underestimated due to diagnostic challenges.
Classification of PIDs involves the defective immune arm and defect mechanism; overlaps exist.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases

items.title: Clinical Consequences of Primary Immunodeficiencies (PIDs)
items.content: The clinical manifestations of PIDs vary widely, depending on the specific molecules affected. They can lead to broad or specific vulnerabilities to infections and may also cause immunopathologic responses.
keyPoints: Vulnerability to infection ranges from broad (e.g., SCID) to narrow (e.g., MSMD).
Infections in PIDs can be caused by pathogenic or opportunistic microorganisms.
PIDs may result in allergy, lymphoproliferation, and autoimmunity.
Some PIDs increase the risk of cancer, particularly lymphocytic cancers like lymphoma.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases

items.title: Integration of Primary Immunodeficiencies with Autoinflammatory Syndromes
items.content: PIDs are now considered part of a broader category of inborn errors of immunity, which also encompasses autoinflammatory syndromes.
keyPoints: PIDs are integrated into the larger group of inborn errors of immunity.
This group includes both PIDs and autoinflammatory syndromes.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES | PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES OF THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | SEVERE CONGENITAL NEUTROPENIA
items.title: Recognizing Severe Congenital Neutropenia (SCN) and Its Diagnostic Approach
items.content: Severe congenital neutropenia is characterized by critically low neutrophil counts (<500 PMNs/μL), often presenting from birth. It can manifest cyclically with a 3-week periodicity. Diagnosis involves bone marrow examination to identify granulopoiesis block at the promyelocytic stage, and genetic testing for mutations in associated genes.
keyPoints: Neutrophil count <500 PMNs/μL
Inheritance patterns: autosomal dominant, recessive, X-linked
Bone marrow examination reveals granulopoiesis block at promyelocytic stage
21 different gene mutations identified; ELANE mutation accounts for 50% of cases
G-CSF treatment improves neutrophil development but has caveats
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · DIAGNOSIS OF PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES | PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES OF THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | SEVERE CONGENITAL NEUTROPENIA

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES | PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES OF THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | SEVERE CONGENITAL NEUTROPENIA
items.title: Understanding the Treatment and Risks of Severe Congenital Neutropenia (SCN)
items.content: Treatment for SCN involves G-CSF to enhance neutrophil development, with hygiene measures crucial in infancy. However, ELANE mutation cases may be refractory to G-CSF, necessitating HSCT. Additionally, G-CSF treatment can increase leukemia risk due to somatic mutations in the G-CSF receptor gene.
keyPoints: G-CSF improves neutrophil development
Hygiene and prophylactic trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole are essential
ELANE mutation cases may require HSCT
Risk of acute myelogenous leukemia with G-CSF treatment in ELANE mutations
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · DIAGNOSIS OF PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES | PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES OF THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | SEVERE CONGENITAL NEUTROPENIA

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES | PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES OF THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | SEVERE CONGENITAL NEUTROPENIA
items.title: Identifying Infections and Complications in Severe Congenital Neutropenia (SCN)
items.content: Patients with SCN are prone to severe bacterial and fungal infections due to low neutrophil counts. The absence of pus is a hallmark. Infections can progress rapidly, leading to dissemination. Additional immune defects may be present, such as in WHIM syndrome or moesin deficiency.
keyPoints: Common infections: bacterial (soft tissue, bloodstream), fungal
Absence of pus is characteristic
Rapid progression and dissemination of infections
Additional immune defects possible in syndromic cases
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · DIAGNOSIS OF PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES | PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES OF THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | SEVERE CONGENITAL NEUTROPENIA

items.subtopic: ASPLENIA | GATA2 DEFICIENCY | LEUKOCYTE ADHESION DEFICIENCY
items.title: Prophylactic Measures in Asplenia to Prevent Infections
items.content: Asplenia, characterized by the absence of a spleen, leads to increased susceptibility to infections due to impaired filtration of encapsulated bacteria. Prophylactic measures are crucial for preventing life-threatening infections.
keyPoints: Absence of natural filtration predisposes individuals to fulminant infections by encapsulated bacteria.
Most infections occur in the first years of life, but can also arise in adulthood.
Diagnosis confirmed by abdominal ultrasonography and detection of Howell-Jolly bodies in red blood cells.
Prophylactic measures include twice-daily oral penicillin and appropriate vaccination programs to prevent fatal outcomes.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · ASPLENIA | GATA2 DEFICIENCY | LEUKOCYTE ADHESION DEFICIENCY

items.subtopic: ASPLENIA | GATA2 DEFICIENCY | LEUKOCYTE ADHESION DEFICIENCY
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Leukocyte Adhesion Deficiency (LAD)
items.content: Leukocyte adhesion deficiency (LAD) involves defects in leukocyte migration, leading to severe infections and impaired wound healing. Diagnosis is based on clinical presentation and specific laboratory findings.
keyPoints: LAD consists of three autosomal recessive conditions: LAD I, II, III.
LAD I caused by mutations in the $\beta2$ integrin gene; LAD III involves a defect in kindlin (Fermt 3).
Susceptibility to bacterial and fungal infections similar to SCN due to impaired neutrophil migration.
Diagnosis suspected with pus-free skin/tissue infections, massive hyperleukocytosis (>30,000/$\mu$L), and delayed loss of umbilical cord.
LAD III also causes bleeding due to non-functional $\beta2$ integrin in platelets.
Immunofluorescence and functional assays for $\beta2$ integrin aid diagnosis.
Severe LAD may require HSCT; gene therapy is being considered. Anti-IL-17A antibody therapy can mitigate inflammatory consequences.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · ASPLENIA | GATA2 DEFICIENCY | LEUKOCYTE ADHESION DEFICIENCY

items.title: Understanding the Etiology and Inheritance Patterns of CGD
items.content: Chronic granulomatous diseases (CGDs) are primarily caused by defective production of reactive oxygen species due to mutations affecting components of NADPH oxidase. This defect impairs phagocytic killing, leading to recurrent infections.
keyPoints: Incidence is ~1 per 200,000 live births.
70% of cases follow X-linked recessive inheritance; 30% are autosomal.
Defective ROS production in the phagolysosome due to NADPH oxidase component mutations.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · CHRONIC GRANULOMATOUS DISEASES

items.title: Management Strategies for CGD: Infection Prevention and Treatment
items.content: Management of CGD focuses on preventing infections through prophylactic antibiotics and antifungals, treating acute infections with combination therapies, and considering hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) as a curative approach.
keyPoints: Prophylactic trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole reduces bacterial infection risk.
Daily azole derivatives like itraconazole decrease fungal complications.
HSCT is an established curative option, though the risk-versus-benefit ratio must be assessed individually.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · CHRONIC GRANULOMATOUS DISEASES

items.subtopic: MENDELIAN SUSCEPTIBILITY TO MYCOBACTERIAL DISEASE | TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR (TLR) PATHWAY DEFICIENCIES
items.title: Recognize Mendelian Susceptibility to Mycobacterial Disease (MSMD) in Persistent Infections
items.content: Patients with persistent mycobacterial infections, including both tuberculous and nontuberculous forms, may have a defect in the IL-12-interferon gamma axis. This condition is characterized by impaired macrophage activation due to deficiencies in components like IL-12p40, IL-12 receptor subunits, IFN-gamma receptors, TYK2, STAT1, IRF8, and ISG515. MSMD can present with severe disseminated infections that are life-threatening despite appropriate antimycobacterial therapy.
keyPoints: Defect in the IL-12-interferon gamma axis leads to impaired macrophage activation.
MSMD patients have a specific vulnerability to mycobacterial and Salmonella infections.
Consider MSMD in any patient with persistent mycobacterial infection.
Treatment options include IFN-gamma therapy for IL-12/IL-12R deficiency and HSCT for severe cases.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · MENDELIAN SUSCEPTIBILITY TO MYCOBACTERIAL DISEASE | TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR (TLR) PATHWAY DEFICIENCIES

items.subtopic: MENDELIAN SUSCEPTIBILITY TO MYCOBACTERIAL DISEASE | TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR (TLR) PATHWAY DEFICIENCIES
items.title: Understand TLR Pathway Deficiencies and Their Clinical Implications
items.content: Patients with early-onset, invasive infections by Streptococcus pneumoniae or Staphylococcus aureus may have recessive mutations in genes like IRAK4 and MYD88, which are crucial for TLR signaling. These deficiencies can lead to decreased susceptibility to infections after the first few years of life due to adaptive immunity development. Specific TLR deficiencies (TLR-3, -7, -8, -9) can result in severe viral infections such as herpes simplex encephalitis and increased risk of severe COVID-19.
keyPoints: Recessive mutations in IRAK4 and MYD88 affect TLR signaling, leading to early-onset invasive infections.
Susceptibility decreases after initial years due to adaptive immunity development.
Deficiencies in TLR-3, -7, -8, -9 can cause severe viral infections like herpes simplex encephalitis.
TLR7 deficiency is a risk factor for severe COVID-19; autoantibodies against IFN-alpha or beta are also linked to severe COVID-19.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · MENDELIAN SUSCEPTIBILITY TO MYCOBACTERIAL DISEASE | TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR (TLR) PATHWAY DEFICIENCIES

items.title: Why Complement Deficiencies Lead to Specific Infections?
items.content: Deficiencies in the complement system, particularly components of the classic pathway (C1q, C1r, C1s, C4, and C2) or factor I, predispose individuals to tissue-invasive bacterial infections and respiratory tract infections. This is due to impaired opsonization and phagocytosis. Deficiencies in alternative pathway components (factors D and properdin) increase susceptibility to invasive Neisseria infections.
keyPoints: Classic pathway deficiencies lead to vulnerability to tissue-invasive or respiratory bacterial infections.
C3 deficiency or factor I deficiency results in similar infection risks due to impaired opsonization.
Alternative pathway deficiencies (factors D and properdin) predispose to invasive Neisseria infections.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · COMPLEMENT DEFICIENCY

items.title: How to Diagnose and Manage Complement Deficiencies?
items.content: Diagnosis of complement deficiencies involves functional assays such as the CH50 test for the classic pathway and the AP50 test for the alternate pathway. When these pathways are impaired, specific component testing is done for precise diagnosis. Management includes appropriate vaccinations and daily oral penicillin to prevent recurrent infections.
keyPoints: CH50 and AP50 tests diagnose functional impairments in the classic and alternate pathways, respectively.
Profound impairment requires determination of specific component status for precise diagnosis.
Preventive management with vaccinations and daily oral penicillin is effective against recurrent infections.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · COMPLEMENT DEFICIENCY

items.title: Importance of Early Diagnosis and Intervention in T Lymphocyte Deficiencies
items.content: T lymphocytes play a crucial role in adaptive immune responses, and deficiencies can lead to severe pathologic consequences. Early diagnosis and intervention are essential for improving prognosis.
keyPoints: T-cell deficiencies account for ~20% of all cases of PID.
Early diagnosis and appropriate therapy are critical due to poor overall prognosis.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics and Consequences of Severe Combined Immunodeficiencies (SCID)
items.content: SCIDs involve a profound block in T-cell development, leading to the complete absence of these cells. They manifest early in life with severe clinical consequences.
keyPoints: Incidence of SCID is estimated at 1 in 50,000 live births.
Clinical manifestations include recurrent oral candidiasis, failure to thrive, and protracted diarrhea within 3–6 months of birth.
Lymphocytopenia strongly suggests SCID in >90% of cases.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Approaches for SCID
items.content: Diagnosis of SCID involves clinical history, family history, and laboratory tests. Screening methods like TREC quantification are reliable for newborn screening.
keyPoints: Lymphocytopenia is a key diagnostic indicator.
Absence of thymic shadow on chest x-ray can suggest SCID.
TREC quantification by Guthrie card is a reliable diagnostic test for newborn screening.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 1)

items.title: SCID due to Cytokine Signaling Deficiency
items.content: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID) can result from deficiencies in cytokine signaling pathways, particularly involving the common gamma chain ($\gamma$ chain) or Jak-associated kinase (JAK) 3. The absence of both T and NK cells is a frequent phenotype, with $\gamma$ deficiency being X-linked and JAK 3 deficiency autosomal recessive.
keyPoints: 30–40% of SCID cases involve the absence of both T and NK cells due to cytokine signaling deficiencies.
$\gamma$ chain deficiency has an X-linked inheritance pattern, while JAK 3 deficiency is autosomal recessive.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 2)

items.title: SCID due to Purine Metabolism Deficiency
items.content: Adenosine deaminase (ADA) deficiency accounts for 10–20% of SCID cases and leads to the accumulation of toxic metabolites, causing lymphocyte progenitor cell death. This results in a lack of B, NK, and T cells. ADA deficiency can also cause bone dysplasia and neurologic defects.
keyPoints: ADA deficiency affects 10–20% of SCID patients.
Bone dysplasia occurs in 50% of cases with ADA deficiency.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 2)

items.title: SCID due to Defective T- and B-cell Receptor Rearrangements
items.content: Deficiencies in the recombination of V(D)J gene elements lead to SCID with selective T and B lymphocyte deficiencies. These conditions, accounting for 20–30% of cases, involve mutations in genes like RAG1, RAG2, DNA-dependent protein kinase, and Artemis.
keyPoints: Defective receptor rearrangements account for 20–30% of SCID cases.
Key gene deficiencies include RAG1, RAG2, DNA-dependent protein kinase, and Artemis.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 2)

items.title: Omenn Syndrome: Unique Presentation and Management
items.content: Omenn syndrome is a subset of T-cell deficiencies characterized by early-onset erythroderma, alopecia, hepatosplenomegaly, and failure to thrive. Patients exhibit T-cell lymphocytosis, eosinophilia, low B-cell counts, and low TCR heterogeneity due to hypomorphic mutations in genes like RAG1, RAG2, ARTEMIS, or IL-7Ra.
keyPoints: Omenn syndrome presents with erythroderma, alopecia, hepatosplenomegaly, failure to thrive
Characterized by T-cell lymphocytosis, eosinophilia, low B-cell counts, and low TCR heterogeneity
Caused by hypomorphic mutations in RAG1, RAG2, ARTEMIS, or IL-7Ra
Management includes anti-infective therapy, nutritional support, immunosuppression, and HSCT as a curative approach
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 3)

items.title: ZAP70 Deficiency: Impact on T-cell Activation
items.content: ZAP70 deficiency leads to a near-complete absence of CD8+ T cells and non-functional CD4+ T cells due to impaired activation by TCR stimulation. This results in susceptibility to infections, chronic diarrhea, failure to thrive, and autoimmune manifestations.
keyPoints: ZAP70 deficiency causes almost complete absence of CD8+ T cells
CD4+ T cells present but cannot be activated by TCR stimulation
Results in viral and opportunistic infections, chronic diarrhea, failure to thrive, and autoimmunity
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 3)

items.title: HLA Class II Deficiency: Consequences and Susceptibilities
items.content: Defective expression of HLA class II molecules due to mutations in RFX5, RFXAP, RFXANK, or CIITA leads to low CD4+ T-cell counts and defective antigen-specific responses. Patients are particularly susceptible to herpesvirus, adenovirus, enterovirus infections, and chronic Cryptosporidium infections.
keyPoints: HLA class II deficiency results from mutations in RFX5, RFXAP, RFXANK, or CIITA
Characterized by low CD4+ T-cell counts and defective antigen-specific responses
Increased susceptibility to herpesvirus, adenovirus, enterovirus, and chronic Cryptosporidium infections
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 3)

items.title: AT and AT-like Disease: Genetic Mutations and High Malignancy Risk
items.content: Ataxia-telangiectasia (AT) is a progressive disease caused by mutations in the ATM gene, leading to DNA repair defects. A variant, AT-like disease, results from MRE11 gene mutations. Both conditions are associated with high risks of lymphoma and leukemia.
keyPoints: Diagnosis of AT involves cytogenetic analysis showing excessive chromosomal rearrangements on chromosomes 7 and 14.
AT is caused by a mutation in the ATM gene, crucial for DNA repair.
AT-like disease results from mutations in the MRE11 gene.
Both conditions carry high risks of malignancies like lymphoma and leukemia.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 4)

items.title: Nijmegen Breakage Syndrome: Chromosome Instability and Immune Deficiency
items.content: Nijmegen breakage syndrome (NBS) is characterized by severe T- and B-cell immune deficiency, microcephaly, and a bird-like face. It results from mutations affecting nibrin, leading to chromosome instability similar to AT.
keyPoints: NBS has autosomal recessive inheritance with severe combined immune deficiency.
Patients exhibit microcephaly and a bird-like facial appearance without ataxia or telangiectasia.
High risk of malignancies due to chromosomal instability.
Caused by mutations in the NBS1 gene affecting nibrin.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 4)

items.title: Autosomal Dominant Hyper-IgE Syndrome: Clinical Features and Genetic Cause
items.content: Autosomal dominant hyper-IgE syndrome is marked by recurrent skin and lung infections, facial dysmorphia, and elevated IgE levels. It results from a heterozygous mutation in the STAT3 gene affecting cytokine signaling.
keyPoints: Characterized by recurrent pyogenic bacterial and fungal infections.
Associated with facial dysmorphy, defective tooth loss, hyperextensibility, scoliosis, and osteoporosis.
Elevated serum IgE levels are typical.
Caused by a heterozygous mutation in the STAT3 gene.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 4)

items.title: Hyper-IgM Syndrome: Diagnosis and Management
items.content: Hyper-IgM syndrome (HIGM), previously classified as a B-cell deficiency, results from defective immunoglobulin class switch recombination leading to low levels of IgG, IgA, and IgE while maintaining IgM. It often presents with opportunistic infections such as interstitial pneumonitis by P. jirovecii in young children. The majority of cases are X-linked due to CD40 ligand deficiency, necessitating thorough investigations for all HIGM cases and consideration of potentially curative hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) on a case-by-case basis.
keyPoints: Defective immunoglobulin class switch recombination leads to low IgG, IgA, and IgE but normal IgM levels.
Opportunistic infections include interstitial pneumonitis by P. jirovecii in young children.
Most cases are X-linked due to CD40 ligand deficiency.
Thorough investigations for all HIGM cases are necessary.
HSCT should be considered on a case-by-case basis.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 5)

items.title: Wiskott-Aldrich Syndrome: Clinical Features and Management
items.content: Wiskott-Aldrich Syndrome (WAS), an X-linked recessive disease with an incidence of ~1 in 200,000 live births, is caused by mutations in the WASP gene. It affects T lymphocytes, other lymphocyte subsets, dendritic cells, and platelets, leading to recurrent bacterial infections, eczema, and bleeding due to thrombocytopenia. Null mutations can lead to severe infections, autoimmune manifestations, and lymphoma. Diagnosis is based on intracellular immunofluorescence analysis of WAS protein expression. Management includes prophylactic antibiotics, IgG supplementation, and HSCT, which is curative.
keyPoints: Incidence: ~1 in 200,000 live births.
Caused by mutations in the WASP gene affecting multiple cell types.
Clinical manifestations include recurrent infections, eczema, and thrombocytopenia.
Null mutations can lead to severe infections, autoimmune disorders, and lymphoma.
Diagnosis via intracellular immunofluorescence analysis of WAS protein expression.
Management includes prophylactic antibiotics, IgG supplementation, and HSCT.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 5)

items.title: Chronic Mucocutaneous Candidiasis: Etiology and Associations
items.content: Chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis (CMC) is a heterogeneous disease with various inheritance patterns, often associated with late-onset bronchopulmonary infections, bronchiectasis, and brain aneurysms. Moderate forms are linked to autoimmunity and AIRE deficiency, with predisposition to Candida infection associated with autoantibodies to TH17 cytokines. Deficiencies in IL-17A, IL-17F, IL-17 receptor A and C, Act1, and gain-of-function mutations in STAT1 are related to defective TH17 function.
keyPoints: CMC is heterogeneous with various inheritance patterns.
Associated with late-onset bronchopulmonary infections, bronchiectasis, and brain aneurysms.
Moderate forms linked to autoimmunity and AIRE deficiency.
Predisposition to Candida infection associated with autoantibodies to TH17 cytokines.
Deficiencies in IL-17A, IL-17F, IL-17 receptor A and C, Act1, and STAT1 mutations related to defective TH17 function.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 5)

items.title: Prevalence and Impact of B Lymphocyte Deficiencies
items.content: B lymphocyte deficiencies are the most common primary immune deficiency diseases (PIDs), accounting for 60–70% of all cases. These deficiencies lead to defective antibody production, resulting in recurrent infections such as sinusitis, bronchitis, and pneumonia primarily caused by specific bacteria like S. pneumoniae.
keyPoints: B lymphocyte deficiencies account for 60–70% of PIDs.
Defective antibody production leads to invasive bacterial infections.
Common pathogens include S. pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, and Moraxella catarrhalis.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis of B-cell PIDs
items.content: The diagnosis of B-cell primary immune deficiencies relies on serum Ig level determination and antibody production assessment following immunization with specific antigens. B-cell phenotype analysis is also crucial for diagnosing conditions like agammaglobulinemia.
keyPoints: Serum Ig levels are critical for PID diagnosis.
Immunization tests include tetanus toxoid vaccine and nonconjugated pneumococcal polysaccharide antigens.
B-cell phenotype analysis involves switched (μ-δ-CD27+) and nonswitched memory B cells (μ+ δ+ CD27+).
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics of Agammaglobulinemia
items.content: Agammaglobulinemia is characterized by a profound defect in B-cell development with less than 1% of normal B-cell blood count and very low residual Ig isotypes. It is primarily caused by mutations in the BTK gene, found on the X chromosome, affecting pre-B to B-cell stage transition.
keyPoints: Agammaglobulinemia involves <1% of normal B-cell count.
85% cases are due to BTK gene mutations on the X chromosome.
Diagnosis can involve intracellular immunofluorescence for BTK detection.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 1)

items.title: Hyper-IgM Syndrome: Mechanisms and Clinical Features
items.content: Hyper-IgM syndrome is a rare B-cell primary immunodeficiency disorder characterized by defective class switch recombination (CSR), leading to low serum levels of IgG and IgA, with elevated or normal IgM. It can result from mutations in CD40L or CD40 genes affecting both B and T cells, or isolated mutations in activation-induced deaminase affecting only B cells.
keyPoints: Characterized by defective CSR resulting in low IgG and IgA, elevated or normal IgM
50% of cases involve mutations in the gene encoding activation-induced deaminase
Enlarged lymphoid organs are common in patients with isolated HIGM syndromes
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 2)

items.title: Common Variable Immunodeficiency: Diagnosis and Genetic Associations
items.content: CVID is an ill-defined condition with low serum levels of one or more Ig isotypes, affecting adults predominantly but can occur earlier in life. It has a prevalence of 1 in 20,000. A family history is found in 10% of cases, with some showing dominant inheritance patterns.
keyPoints: Prevalence estimated at 1 in 20,000
Family history present in 10% of cases
Mutations in ICOS and TACI genes associated with CVID
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 2)

items.title: Selective Ig Isotype Deficiencies: Clinical Spectrum and Progression
items.content: IgA deficiency is the most common primary immunodeficiency, found in 1 in every 600 individuals, often asymptomatic but can lead to respiratory infections and autoimmune diseases. It may progress to CVID, necessitating serum Ig level assessments.
keyPoints: IgA deficiency prevalence: 1 in 600
Often asymptomatic but associated with increased risk of infections and autoimmune diseases
May progress to CVID; important to monitor serum Ig levels
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 2)

items.title: Selective Antibody Deficiency to Polysaccharide Antigens
items.content: Patients with normal serum Ig levels may still be prone to infections by S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae due to defective antibody production against polysaccharide antigens, likely linked to marginal zone B cell defects.
keyPoints: Defective antibody response against polysaccharides like those in S. pneumoniae can cause respiratory tract infections despite normal serum Ig levels.
This condition is associated with a defect in marginal zone B cells responsible for T-independent antibody responses.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 3)

items.title: Immunoglobulin Replacement Therapy
items.content: IgG replacement therapy is guided by infection occurrence rather than lab data alone to prevent unjustified infusions. It aims to reduce respiratory infections and chronic lung disease, with a target trough level above 800 mg/mL.
keyPoints: IgG antibodies have a half-life of 21–28 days.
Immunoglobulin replacement should be guided by infection occurrence, not just lab data.
Target trough IgG level is above 800 mg/mL for effective therapy.
IV injections are repeated every 3-4 weeks; subcutaneous once weekly or adjusted based on patient needs.
Both IV and subcutaneous routes are equally safe and efficacious.
Patients with chronic lung disease may need additional treatments like chest physical therapy and azithromycin.
IgG preparations contain small amounts of IgA, so caution is needed for patients with complete IgA deficiency to avoid anaphylactic shock.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 3)

items.subtopic: PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES AFFECTING REGULATORY PATHWAYS (TABLE 362-1) | HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS
items.title: Diagnosis and Initial Management of Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis (HLH)
items.content: HLH is diagnosed based on characteristic symptoms such as fever, hepatosplenomegaly, edema, neurologic diseases, blood cytopenia, increased liver enzymes, hypofibrinogenemia, high triglyceride and ferritin levels, elevated T-cell activation markers, and hemophagocytic features in bone marrow or cerebrospinal fluid. Functional assays like CD107 fluorescence can suggest genetically determined HLH.
keyPoints: Characteristic symptoms include fever, hepatosplenomegaly, edema, neurologic diseases, blood cytopenia, increased liver enzymes, hypofibrinogenemia, high triglyceride and ferritin levels, elevated T-cell activation markers, hemophagocytic features in bone marrow or cerebrospinal fluid.
Functional assays of postactivation cytotoxic granule exocytosis (CD107 fluorescence) can suggest genetically determined HLH.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES AFFECTING REGULATORY PATHWAYS (TABLE 362-1) | HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.subtopic: PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES AFFECTING REGULATORY PATHWAYS (TABLE 362-1) | HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS
items.title: Genetic Subtypes and Their Identification in HLH
items.content: HLH can be classified into three subsets: familial HLH with autosomal recessive inheritance (including perforin deficiency, Munc13-4 deficiency, syntaxin 11 deficiency, Munc18-2 deficiency), HLH with partial albinism (Chédiak-Higashi syndrome, Griscelli syndrome, Hermansky-Pudlak syndrome type II), and X-linked lymphoproliferative disease (XLP) caused by mutations in the SH2DIA gene.
keyPoints: Familial HLH includes perforin deficiency (30% of cases), Munc13-4 deficiency (30%), syntaxin 11 deficiency (10%), Munc18-2 deficiency (20%).
HLH with partial albinism involves Chédiak-Higashi syndrome, Griscelli syndrome, Hermansky-Pudlak syndrome type II.
XLP is caused by mutations in the SH2DIA gene and can present with progressive hypogammaglobulinemia or lymphomas.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES AFFECTING REGULATORY PATHWAYS (TABLE 362-1) | HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.subtopic: PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES AFFECTING REGULATORY PATHWAYS (TABLE 362-1) | HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS
items.title: Treatment and Prognosis of HLH
items.content: HLH is a life-threatening condition requiring aggressive immunosuppression with etoposide or anti-T-cell antibodies. Targeting the IFN-γ pathway (JAK1) is an additional treatment option. Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) should be performed once remission is achieved, as it provides the only curative therapy.
keyPoints: Aggressive immunosuppression with etoposide or anti-T-cell antibodies is required.
Targeting the IFN-γ pathway (JAK1) can be considered.
HSCT should be performed post-remission for a curative outcome.

items.subtopic: AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES
items.title: Characteristics and Diagnosis of Autoimmune Lymphoproliferative Syndrome (ALPS)
items.content: Autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome is marked by nonmalignant T and B cell proliferation leading to splenomegaly and enlarged lymph nodes. A significant portion of patients exhibit autoimmune manifestations, including cytopenias and hepatitis. The presence of CD4-CD8-TCRαβ+ T cells in the blood (2–50%) is a diagnostic hallmark.
keyPoints: 70% of ALPS patients display autoimmune manifestations such as cytopenias, Guillain-Barré syndrome, uveitis, and hepatitis.
Presence of CD4-CD8-TCRαβ+ T cells in the blood (2–50%) is diagnostic for ALPS.
ALPS results from a defect in Fas-mediated apoptosis leading to lymphocyte accumulation.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES

items.subtopic: AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES
items.title: Genetic Basis and Treatment of Autoimmune Lymphoproliferative Syndrome (ALPS)
items.content: Most ALPS cases are due to heterozygous mutations in the Fas gene with dominant inheritance. Severe forms may result from biallelic mutations affecting protein expression/function. Treatment involves proapoptotic drugs, which must be administered cautiously.
keyPoints: Heterozygous mutation in the Fas gene is common in ALPS, showing dominant inheritance and variable penetrance.
Severe forms of ALPS can result from biallelic mutations impairing Fas protein expression/function.
Proapoptotic drugs are used for treatment but require careful administration to avoid toxicity.

items.title: IPEX Syndrome: Clinical Features and Management
items.content: Immunodysregulation polyendocrinopathy enteropathy X-linked syndrome (IPEX) is characterized by severe gut inflammation, food intolerance, and autoimmune cytopenias due to FOXP3 mutations. Absence of CD4+CD25+ regulatory T cells in the blood is common, necessitating aggressive immunosuppression or allogeneic HSCT for management.
keyPoints: IPEX syndrome results from loss-of-function mutations in FOXP3.
Characterized by severe gut inflammation, food intolerance, and autoimmune cytopenias.
Management includes aggressive immunosuppression or curative allogeneic HSCT.

items.title: Importance of Early Diagnosis and Referral for PIDs
items.content: Early diagnosis of Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases (PIDs) is crucial to establish an appropriate therapeutic regimen. Patients with suspected PIDs should be referred to experienced clinical centers capable of performing molecular and genetic tests.
keyPoints: Early diagnosis is essential for establishing an appropriate therapeutic regimen.
Referral to experienced clinical centers for molecular and genetic testing is necessary.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · CONCLUSION

items.title: Challenges in Diagnosing PIDs Due to Variability
items.content: Diagnosis of PIDs can be complicated by high variability in disease expression, which may result from different mutations, modifier genes, environmental factors, and somatic mutations. Cytokine-neutralizing autoantibodies can also mimic PID.
keyPoints: High variability in disease expression due to various mutations (e.g., WAS, XLA, CTLA4 deficiency).
Modifier genes and environmental factors like EBV infection can influence disease expression.
Somatic mutations can attenuate or create disease states analogous to PIDs (e.g., ADA deficiency, X-linked SCID, RAG deficiencies, NEMO deficiency, WAS, ALPS, VEXAS syndrome).
Cytokine-neutralizing autoantibodies can mimic PID (e.g., IFN-γ, IL-6, IL-7, type I interferon).

items.question: Which of the following is a hallmark of Autoimmune Lymphoproliferative Syndrome (ALPS)?
options: Presence of CD4+CD8- TCRαβ+ T cells in the blood
Presence of CD4-CD8- TCRαβ+ T cells in the blood
Absence of B cells in peripheral blood
items.explanation: A hallmark of ALPS is the presence of CD4-CD8- TCRαβ+ T cells (2–50%) in the blood.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES

items.subtopic: AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES
items.question: What is the primary cause of ALPS?
options: Defect in Fas-mediated apoptosis of lymphocytes
items.explanation: The syndrome is caused by a defect in Fas-mediated apoptosis of lymphocytes.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES

items.subtopic: AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES
items.question: Which treatment is primarily used for ALPS?
items.explanation: Treatment of ALPS is essentially based on the use of proapoptotic drugs.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES

items.subtopic: AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES
items.question: Which gene mutation is associated with a B cell-predominant form of ALPS?
items.explanation: A B cell-predominant ALPS has recently been found associated with a protein kinase Cδ gene mutation.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES

items.question: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a factor contributing to variable disease expression in PIDs?
options: Differing consequences of various mutations associated with a given condition
Environmental factors such as EBV infection
Somatic mutations creating disease states analogous to PID
items.explanation: While somatic mutations can create disease states analogous to PIDs, they are not mentioned as contributing to variable disease expression.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · CONCLUSION

items.question: What is the significance of a precise molecular diagnosis in patients with suspected PIDs?
options: It helps in identifying environmental triggers.
It is necessary for initiating the most suitable treatment and genetic counseling.
It confirms the presence of cytokine-neutralizing autoantibodies.
It identifies all disease-causing gene mutations.
items.explanation: A precise molecular diagnosis is crucial for starting appropriate treatment and providing genetic counseling.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · CONCLUSION

items.question: What can somatic mutations in an affected gene do to the phenotype of T-cell PIDs?
options: They always worsen the disease phenotype.
They have no effect on the disease phenotype.
They are responsible for all cases of PID.
items.explanation: Somatic mutations in an affected gene can attenuate the phenotype of a number of T-cell PIDs.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · CONCLUSION

items.statement: PIDs are primarily caused by environmental factors.
items.explanation: PIDs are genetic diseases with primarily Mendelian inheritance, not caused by environmental factors.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases

items.statement: The classification of PIDs sometimes involves arbitrary decisions due to overlap and lack of data.
items.explanation: Classification of PIDs can be arbitrary because of overlap in symptoms and lack of comprehensive data.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES | PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES OF THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | SEVERE CONGENITAL NEUTROPENIA
items.statement: Severe congenital neutropenia (SCN) is characterized by elevated neutrophil counts.
items.explanation: SCN is characterized by severely impaired neutrophil counts (<500 PMN/μL of blood).
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · DIAGNOSIS OF PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES | PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES OF THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | SEVERE CONGENITAL NEUTROPENIA

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES | PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES OF THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | SEVERE CONGENITAL NEUTROPENIA
items.statement: SCN can be associated with a block in granulopoiesis at the promyelocytic stage.
items.explanation: Most SCNs are associated with a block in granulopoiesis at the promyelocytic stage.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · DIAGNOSIS OF PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES | PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES OF THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | SEVERE CONGENITAL NEUTROPENIA

items.subtopic: ASPLENIA | GATA2 DEFICIENCY | LEUKOCYTE ADHESION DEFICIENCY
items.statement: GATA2 deficiency can lead to acute myeloid leukemia.
items.explanation: GATA2 deficiency predisposes individuals to acute myeloid leukemia.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · ASPLENIA | GATA2 DEFICIENCY | LEUKOCYTE ADHESION DEFICIENCY

items.subtopic: ASPLENIA | GATA2 DEFICIENCY | LEUKOCYTE ADHESION DEFICIENCY
items.statement: Leukocyte adhesion deficiency type III (LAD III) does not cause bleeding issues.
items.explanation: LAD III causes bleeding due to non-functional β2 integrin in platelets.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · ASPLENIA | GATA2 DEFICIENCY | LEUKOCYTE ADHESION DEFICIENCY

items.statement: Prophylactic usage of trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole is effective in reducing the risk of bacterial infections in CGD patients.
items.explanation: Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis is well tolerated and highly effective for preventing bacterial infections.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · CHRONIC GRANULOMATOUS DISEASES

items.statement: Chronic Granulomatous Diseases (CGD) are primarily caused by overproduction of reactive oxygen species.
items.explanation: CGD is caused by defective production, not overproduction, of ROS.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · CHRONIC GRANULOMATOUS DISEASES

items.subtopic: MENDELIAN SUSCEPTIBILITY TO MYCOBACTERIAL DISEASE | TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR (TLR) PATHWAY DEFICIENCIES
items.statement: Susceptibility to infection in patients with TLR pathway deficiencies decreases after the first few years of life.
items.explanation: Adaptive immunity triggered by initial microbial challenges may prevent recurrent infections.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · MENDELIAN SUSCEPTIBILITY TO MYCOBACTERIAL DISEASE | TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR (TLR) PATHWAY DEFICIENCIES

items.statement: No other TLR deficiencies have been found despite extensive screening of patients with unexplained, recurrent infections.
items.explanation: This suggests that these receptors are functionally redundant.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · MENDELIAN SUSCEPTIBILITY TO MYCOBACTERIAL DISEASE | TOLL-LIKE RECEPTOR (TLR) PATHWAY DEFICIENCIES

items.statement: A deficiency in the classic pathway components (C1q, C1r, C1s, C4, and C2) predisposes individuals to invasive Neisseria infections.
items.explanation: Deficiencies in the classic pathway predispose to bacterial infections that are tissue-invasive or occur in the respiratory tract, not specifically Neisseria.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · COMPLEMENT DEFICIENCY

items.statement: Complement deficiencies can predispose individuals to autoimmune diseases such as systemic lupus erythematosus.
items.explanation: Several complement deficiencies (in the classic pathway and the lytic phase) may predispose affected individuals to autoimmune diseases like systemic lupus erythematosus.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · COMPLEMENT DEFICIENCY

items.statement: The absence of a thymic shadow on a chest x-ray is strongly suggestive of SCID.
items.explanation: Absence of a thymic shadow can be indicative of SCID.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 1)

items.statement: SCIDs are characterized by a profound block in B-cell development.
items.explanation: SCID is characterized by a profound block in T-cell development.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 1)

items.statement: Gene therapy has been successful for treating X-linked SCID caused by $\gamma$ chain deficiency.
items.explanation: Gene therapy is a successful treatment option for X-linked SCID due to $\gamma$ chain deficiency.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 2)

items.statement: DiGeorge's syndrome always results in a complete absence of the thymus.
items.explanation: In DiGeorge's syndrome, the thymus is completely absent only in about 1% of cases.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 2)

items.statement: Patients with HLA Class I Deficiency typically exhibit chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and severe vasculitis.
items.explanation: HLA Class I Deficiency leads to these specific phenotypic manifestations.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 3)

items.statement: HSCT is not indicated for most T-cell primary immunodeficiencies.
items.explanation: HSCT is indeed indicated for most of these diseases.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 3)

items.statement: Nijmegen breakage syndrome is characterized by ataxia.
items.explanation: Individuals with NBS do not exhibit ataxia; they have microcephaly and a bird-like face.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 4)

items.statement: Autosomal dominant hyper-IgE syndrome is caused by a mutation in the STAT3 gene.
items.explanation: The condition results from a heterozygous mutation in the STAT3 gene.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 4)

items.statement: Wiskott-Aldrich Syndrome is an autosomal recessive disease.
items.explanation: Wiskott-Aldrich Syndrome is a complex, recessive, X-linked disease.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 5)

items.statement: Hyper-IgM syndrome can result from a deficiency in CD40 ligand.
items.explanation: Approximately half of HIGM cases are due to a deficiency in CD40 ligand.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 5)

items.statement: Infections rarely occur before the age of 6 months in patients with profound B-cell deficiencies.
items.explanation: Transient protection from maternal immunoglobulins prevents infections until after 6 months.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 1)

items.statement: All mutations in the BTK gene result in agammaglobulinemia.
items.explanation: Not all BTK mutations lead to agammaglobulinemia; some cause milder hypogammaglobulinemia.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 1)

items.statement: CVID is characterized by elevated serum levels of IgG and IgA.
items.explanation: CVID is characterized by low serum levels of one or more Ig isotypes.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 2)

items.statement: IgA deficiency can progress to CVID.
items.explanation: Symptomatic IgA deficiency is related to CVID and may progress to it.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 2)

items.statement: Immunoglobulin replacement therapy should be based solely on laboratory data such as IgG and/or antibody deficiency.
items.explanation: Therapy should not be based on lab data alone but guided by the occurrence of infections.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 3)

items.statement: Immunoglobulin replacement therapy is a lifelong treatment that requires understanding and strict adherence by the patient and their family.
items.explanation: The rationale and procedures of immunoglobulin replacement must be fully understood for efficacy.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 3)

items.subtopic: PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES AFFECTING REGULATORY PATHWAYS (TABLE 362-1) | HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS
items.statement: HLH is primarily caused by bacterial infections.
items.explanation: Viral infections, particularly EBV, are the most frequent triggers for HLH.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES AFFECTING REGULATORY PATHWAYS (TABLE 362-1) | HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.subtopic: PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES AFFECTING REGULATORY PATHWAYS (TABLE 362-1) | HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS
items.statement: HSCT is the only curative therapy for HLH.
items.explanation: HSCT provides the only curative form of therapy once remission has been achieved in HLH.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES AFFECTING REGULATORY PATHWAYS (TABLE 362-1) | HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.subtopic: AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES
items.statement: IPEX syndrome is caused by mutations in the gene encoding Fas.
items.explanation: IPEX syndrome is caused by loss-of-function mutations in the FOXP3 gene.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES

items.subtopic: AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES
items.statement: AIRE gene mutations cause APECED syndrome.
items.explanation: APECED syndrome is due to mutations in the autoimmune regulator (AIRE) gene.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES

items.statement: Cytokine-neutralizing autoantibodies can mimic primary immune deficiency diseases.
items.explanation: Autoantibodies against cytokines like IFN-γ, IL-6, and others can present with symptoms similar to PIDs.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · CONCLUSION

items.statement: The disease-causing gene mutations have been identified in all cases of primary immune deficiency diseases.
items.explanation: Gene mutations causing some PIDs, such as CVID and IgA deficiency, remain unidentified.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · CONCLUSION

items.assertion: The consequences of PIDs vary widely based on the defective molecules.
items.reason: This variation translates into multiple levels of vulnerability to infection by pathogenic and opportunistic microorganisms.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the consequences of PIDs vary.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases

items.assertion: PIDs can lead to immunopathologic responses such as allergy.
items.reason: This is primarily due to defects in adaptive immune responses.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not fully explain why PIDs can lead to immunopathologic responses.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES | PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES OF THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | SEVERE CONGENITAL NEUTROPENIA
items.assertion: SCN exposes patients to life-threatening bacterial and fungal infections.
items.reason: The absence of pus in SCN leads to rapid progression and dissemination of infections.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · DIAGNOSIS OF PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES | PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES OF THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | SEVERE CONGENITAL NEUTROPENIA

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES | PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES OF THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | SEVERE CONGENITAL NEUTROPENIA
items.assertion: G-CSF treatment is effective for all SCN patients.
items.reason: SCN with ELANE mutation may be refractory to G-CSF and require alternative treatments.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because not all SCN patients respond to G-CSF, especially those with certain ELANE mutations.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · DIAGNOSIS OF PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES | PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES OF THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | SEVERE CONGENITAL NEUTROPENIA

items.subtopic: ASPLENIA | GATA2 DEFICIENCY | LEUKOCYTE ADHESION DEFICIENCY
items.assertion: Patients with leukocyte adhesion deficiency type I (LAD I) have impaired wound healing.
items.reason: LAD I results from mutations in the β2 integrin gene affecting leukocyte adhesion to inflamed endothelium.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · ASPLENIA | GATA2 DEFICIENCY | LEUKOCYTE ADHESION DEFICIENCY

items.assertion: Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) can be a curative treatment for CGD.
items.reason: HSCT has been shown to increase the success rate in managing CGD, making it an established approach.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why HSCT is considered curative.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · CHRONIC GRANULOMATOUS DISEASES

items.assertion: SCID can be diagnosed based on lymphocytopenia in more than 90% of cases.
items.reason: Lymphocytopenia is strongly suggestive of SCID due to the profound T-cell deficiency.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 1)

items.assertion: NKT-cell deficiency is associated with X-linked proliferative syndrome.
items.reason: Certain T-cell deficiencies can contribute to the diagnosis of specific syndromes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 1)

items.assertion: SCID patients with ADA deficiency often exhibit bone dysplasia.
items.reason: ADA is a ubiquitous enzyme and its deficiency can lead to abnormal costochondral junctions and metaphyses.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 2)

items.assertion: Reticular dysgenesis is caused by a deficiency in adenosine deaminase.
items.reason: Reticular dysgenesis results from an adenylate kinase 2 deficiency.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; reticular dysgenesis is not caused by ADA deficiency but by adenylate kinase 2 deficiency.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 2)

items.assertion: Ataxia-telangiectasia is associated with low IgA and IgG2 deficiency.
items.reason: It results from B-cell defects due to DNA fragility.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 3)

items.assertion: Calcium signaling defects are caused by mutations in RFX5.
items.reason: These defects result from defective antigen receptor-mediated Ca2+ influx.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; calcium signaling defects are due to ORAI1 or STIM-1, not RFX5.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 3)

items.assertion: Ataxia-telangiectasia is associated with a high risk of malignancies.
items.reason: AT is caused by mutations in the ATM gene which plays a role in DNA repair.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why AT carries a high risk of malignancies.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 4)

items.assertion: Cartilage hair hypoplasia is characterized by elevated serum IgE levels.
items.reason: CHH involves mutations in the RMRP gene affecting ribosome-associated RNA.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; CHH does not involve elevated serum IgE levels.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 4)

items.assertion: Patients with Wiskott-Aldrich Syndrome often have autoimmune manifestations.
items.reason: Null mutations in the WASP gene predispose individuals to autoimmune disorders.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 5)

items.assertion: Gene therapy is not a viable treatment option for Wiskott-Aldrich Syndrome.
items.reason: Gene therapy trials have been performed with success in WAS.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true as gene therapy has shown successful results.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · T LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIGS. 362-2 AND 362-3) (part 5)

items.assertion: Agammaglobulinemia is characterized by a profound defect in B-cell development.
items.reason: The BTK gene mutation leads to a block at the pre-B to B-cell stage.
items.explanation: Both are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 1)

items.assertion: Agammaglobulinemia can be observed in patients with ICF syndrome.
items.reason: ICF syndrome is associated with normal peripheral B-cell counts.
items.explanation: Both are true, but the reason does not explain why agammaglobulinemia occurs.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 1)

items.assertion: Hyper-IgM syndrome can result from fetal rubella syndrome.
items.reason: Fetal rubella syndrome is associated with various immunologic abnormalities.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 2)

items.assertion: Selective IgG2 (+G4) deficiency is always associated with IgA deficiency.
items.reason: Selective IgG2 (+G4) deficiency can be associated with IgA deficiency in some cases.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the association is not always present, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 2)

items.assertion: Immunoglobulin replacement therapy can be administered via IV or subcutaneous routes.
items.reason: Both routes are equally safe and efficacious, allowing patient preference to guide the choice.
items.explanation: The assertion is true and the reason correctly explains it.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · B LYMPHOCYTE DEFICIENCIES (TABLE 362-1, FIG. 362-4) (part 3)

items.subtopic: PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES AFFECTING REGULATORY PATHWAYS (TABLE 362-1) | HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS
items.assertion: XLP2 is associated with a deficiency of the antiapoptotic molecule XIAP.
items.reason: The pathophysiology of XLP2 may be related to control of inflammation in macrophages due to a functional link between XIAP and NLRC4.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the association.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES AFFECTING REGULATORY PATHWAYS (TABLE 362-1) | HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.subtopic: PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES AFFECTING REGULATORY PATHWAYS (TABLE 362-1) | HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS
items.assertion: HLH can be diagnosed solely based on clinical symptoms.
items.reason: Diagnosis of HLH relies on the identification of characteristic symptoms and functional assays.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because diagnosis also requires functional assays, not just clinical symptoms.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES AFFECTING REGULATORY PATHWAYS (TABLE 362-1) | HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.subtopic: AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES
items.assertion: Patients with ALPS often have autoimmune manifestations.
items.reason: ALPS is characterized by a defect in Fas-mediated apoptosis of lymphocytes leading to autoimmunity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES

items.subtopic: AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES
items.assertion: IPEX syndrome has a poor prognosis and requires aggressive immunosuppression.
items.reason: The condition is caused by mutations in the CTLA-4 gene.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; IPEX syndrome is due to FOXP3 gene mutations.
items.reference: Primary Immune Deficiency Diseases · AUTOIMMUNE LYMPHOPROLIFERATIVE SYNDROME | COLITIS, AUTOIMMUNITY, AND PRIMARY IMMUNODEFICIENCIES
